phoneme_inventory.vowel_count.note: "Japanese has 5 distinct vowels: /a, e, i, o, u/" — Wikipedia: Japanese phonology

phoneme_inventory.vowel_length_contrasti.note: "all vowels display a length contrast, where short vowels are phonemically distinct from long vowels" — Wikipedia: Japanese phonology

phoneme_inventory.consonant_gemination_c.note: "consonant length is a distinctive feature in Japanese" — Wikipedia: Japanese phonology

phoneme_inventory.moraic_nasal_is_separa.note: "The n in Honda should have mora duration as well, making the word 3 moras long" — Wikipedia: Mora (linguistics)

mora_timing.timing_unit.note: "Japanese has traditionally been described as exhibiting 'mora timing'" — Wikipedia: Mora (linguistics)

mora_timing.long_vowel_extra_unit.note: "the second part of a long vowel are also considered moras on their own... Tookyoo has 4 moras" — Wikipedia: Mora (linguistics)

mora_timing.sokuon_counts_as_unit.note: "The sokuon indicates a one-mora period of silence" — Wikipedia: Japanese phonology

mora_timing.n_counts_as_unit.note: "moraic nasal (hatsuon)... Honda... 3 moras long, although it has only two syllables" — Wikipedia: Mora (linguistics)

rendaku.applies_to.note: "modifies the consonant at the start of the second (or later) part of the compound" — Wikipedia: Rendaku

rendaku.change_direction.note: "replaces a voiceless consonant, such as /k s t h/, with a voiced consonant, such as /g z d b/" — Wikipedia: Rendaku

rendaku.blocked_by_voiced_obstruent_in_s.note: "Lyman's law... prohibits voicing when the second element already contains a voiced obstruent" — Wikipedia: Rendaku

rendaku.fully_predictable.note: "A sufficient condition for predicting rendaku is not known... only tendencies" — Wikipedia: Rendaku

kana_orthography.particle_topic_spelled.note: "particles (o, e, and wa) continue to be written as を, へ, and は" — Wikipedia: Modern kana usage

kana_orthography.particle_direction_spel.note: "The directional particle へ is today pronounced 'e', though not written as え" — Wikipedia: Modern kana usage

kana_orthography.particle_object_spelled.note: "を... originally assigned as wo, now usually pronounced o" (object marker) — Wikipedia: Modern kana usage

kana_orthography.long_o_hiragana_default.note: "う is used, in its full-sized form, to lengthen 'o' sounds. 構想 → こうそう" — Wikipedia: U (kana)

kana_orthography.sokuon_symbol.note: "sokuon, visually identical to a small tsu, indicates that the following consonant is geminated" — Wikipedia: Sokuon

kana_orthography.katakana_long_vowel_mar.note: "A chōon indicates a long vowel of two morae in length" — Wikipedia: Chōonpu

loanword_katakana.script_for_loanwords.note: "katakana is most often used for transcription of words from foreign languages or loanwords... gairaigo" — Wikipedia: Katakana

loanword_katakana.v_sound_kana.note: "/v/ is transcribed either to b or v, e.g. ラブ ra-bu / ラヴ ravu 'love'" — Wikipedia: Transcription into Japanese

loanword_katakana.transliteration_fully_.note: "transcribed either to b or v... convention is relatively new, many older loanwords do not use it" — Wikipedia: Transcription into Japanese

punctuation_layout.sentence_terminator.note: "The kuten (。) is a small circle used as a full stop" — Wikipedia: Japanese punctuation

punctuation_layout.comma.note: "The comma (、, tōten) is used... for marking off separate elements within a sentence" — Wikipedia: Japanese punctuation

punctuation_layout.quote_marks.note: "「」 are used as quotation marks in the Japanese language" — Wikipedia: Japanese punctuation

punctuation_layout.words_separated_by_sp.note: "Japanese is normally written without spaces between words" — Wikipedia: Japanese writing system

punctuation_layout.question_mark_require.note: "The question mark (？) is not used in traditional or formal Japanese, but may be used in informal writing" — Wikipedia: Japanese punctuation

word_order.basic.note: "Japanese has SOV word order (subject-object-verb)" — Wikipedia: Japanese grammar

word_order.adposition_type.note: "phrase ends with a postposition, in head-final order, as is seen in Japanese" — Wikipedia: Head-directionality parameter

word_order.modifier_position.note: "subordinated sentences (relative clauses...) always precede what they refer to" — Wikipedia: Head-directionality parameter

word_order.verb_strictly_final_in_neutra.note: "Japanese is per excellence a head final language"; SOV: 'street down walking was man' — Wikipedia: Japanese grammar

conjugation.regular_verb_classes.note: "two main types of verbs: godan verbs... and ichidan verbs" — Wikipedia: Japanese godan and ichidan verbs

conjugation.irregular_verbs.note: "best-known irregular verbs are する suru 'do' and 来る kuru 'come'" — Wikipedia: Japanese irregular verbs

conjugation.i_adjectives_inflect.note: "I-adjectives... may predicate sentences and inflect for past, negative, etc." — Wikipedia: Japanese adjectives

conjugation.na_adjectives_need_copula.note: "Na-adjectives attach to the copula... use 〜な when modifying a noun" — Wikipedia: Japanese adjectives

conjugation.person_number_agreement.note: "Japanese verb conjugations are independent of person, number and gender" — Wikipedia: Japanese conjugation

counters.numeral_needs_counter.note: "counters are measure words used with numbers to count things... added directly after numbers" — Wikipedia: Japanese counter word

counters.counter_people.note: "'nin' (used for people) uses traditional numerals for some numbers" — Wikipedia: Japanese counter word

counters.generic_native_counter.note: "'tsu' is a traditional counter word that can be used as a general counter" — Wikipedia: Japanese counter word

counters.sound_changes_occur.note: "variants... through consonant voicing... and gemination (such as roku as rokku)", roppyaku — Wikipedia: Japanese numerals

honorifics_keigo.main_categories.note: "three main categories: sonkeigo, kenjōgo, and teineigo" — Wikipedia: Honorific speech in Japanese

honorifics_keigo.polite_verb_suffix.note: "Teineigo is characterized by... the verb ending masu (ます)" — Wikipedia: Honorific speech in Japanese

honorifics_keigo.honorific_prefix_exists.note: "use of prefixes such as o (お) and go (ご)" — Wikipedia: Honorific speech in Japanese

honorifics_keigo.sonkeigo_refers_to.note: "used when talking about superiors and customers... not used to talk about oneself" — Wikipedia: Honorific speech in Japanese

honorifics_keigo.kenjougo_refers_to.note: "speaker shows respect... by lowering himself; used when referring to oneself" — Wikipedia: Honorific speech in Japanese

sentence_final_particles.ka_function.note: "か (ka): used to turn a declarative sentence into a question" — Wikipedia: Japanese particles

sentence_final_particles.ne_function.note: "ね (ne) is used to indicate or solicit acknowledgement, agreement or confirmation" — Wikipedia: Japanese particles

sentence_final_particles.yo_function.note: "よ (yo) is assertive... supplying information the listener is believed not to know" — Wikipedia: Japanese particles

sentence_final_particles.gender_associat.note: "わ (wa) has traditionally been associated with feminine speech" — Wikipedia: Sentence-final particle

forms_of_address.neutral_polite_suffix.note: "-san is used as a formal and polite way of speaking to others in general" — Wikipedia: Japanese honorifics

forms_of_address.second_person_pronoun_c.note: "pronoun avoidance... pronouns seen as too direct... offensive or strange"; anata inappropriate to teacher — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Japanese

forms_of_address.teacher_title.note: "Sensei (先生) is used to refer to or address teachers, doctors, politicians, lawyers" — Wikipedia: Sensei
